coredata.dc:title: Headspace single-drop microextraction coupled with microvolume fluorospectrometry for highly sensitive determination of bromide
coredata.dc:creator: García-Figueroa, Adrián
Pena-Pereira, Francisco
Lavilla, Isela
Bendicho, Carlos
coredata.dc:description: This work reports on the development of a novel methodology for bromide determination by combining headspace single-drop microextraction with microvolume fluorospectrometry. The method lies in the in situ generation of bromine, transfer of the volatile to the headspace and trapping/reaction onto a fluorescein-containing aqueous drop exposed to the gas phase. The decrease in the fluorescence intensity enabled the determination of bromide without dilution of the enriched microdrop. Experimental parameters influencing the performance of the method, namely, fluorescence parameters, extractant phase composition, bromine generation conditions and microextraction time, were evaluated and controlled. Under optimal conditions, an enrichment factor of 243 was attained. The limits of detection and quantification achieved under optimal conditions for bromide were found to be 1.4 and 4.4µgL−1, respectively. The intra-day repeatability, expressed as relative standard deviation, was 4.4% (n=6). Besides, the inter-day reproducibility, performed at four different days, was 7.1%. Finally, the developed method was successfully applied to the determination of bromide in different water samples, showing recovery values in the range of 95–110%, and validated against certified reference material BCR-611 (ground water, Br− low level). The proposed method represents a highly convenient approach for monitoring of bromide at very low concentrations.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Bromide determination
Fluorospectrometry
Microvolume analysis
Sample preparation
Single-drop microextraction
Water analysis

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Fluorescence emission spectra of a 5µmolL−1 fluorescein solution using UV, white and blue LEDs as excitation sources.
Effect of the extractant phase composition and pH: (A) DMF content; (B) pH.
Effect of bromine generation conditions: (A) Sulfuric acid concentration; (B) Bromate concentration.
Effect of mass transfer conditions: (A) Temperature; (B) Sample volume; (C) Microextraction time.
Analytical characteristics of the proposed method for determination of bromide and comparison with alternative methodologies.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: DPB, dielectric barrier discharge micro-plasma; CE, capillary electrophoresis FLD, fluorescence detection; GC: gas chromatography GD, gas diffusion unit; HS-SDME, headspace single-drop microextraction; IC, ion chromatography; ICP, inductively coupled plasma; LCW, liquid-core waveguide; LPME, liquid phase microextraction; MS, mass spectrometry; MSFIA, multi-syringe flow injection analysis; OES, optical emission spectrometry; PVG, photochemical vapor generation; TOF, time of flight; UV–vis, UV–visible spectrophotometry

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Recovery studies on different water samples.

head.ce:title: Headspace single-drop microextraction coupled with microvolume fluorospectrometry for highly sensitive determination of bromide

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Analytical and Food Chemistry Department; Faculty of Chemistry, University of Vigo, Campus As Lagoas-Marcosende s/n, 36310 Vigo, Spain

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Analytical and Food Chemistry Department; Faculty of Chemistry, University of Vigo

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This work reports on the development of a novel methodology for bromide determination by combining headspace single-drop microextraction with microvolume fluorospectrometry. The method lies in the in situ generation of bromine, transfer of the volatile to the headspace and trapping/reaction onto a fluorescein-containing aqueous drop exposed to the gas phase. The decrease in the fluorescence intensity enabled the determination of bromide without dilution of the enriched microdrop. Experimental parameters influencing the performance of the method, namely, fluorescence parameters, extractant phase composition, bromine generation conditions and microextraction time, were evaluated and controlled. Under optimal conditions, an enrichment factor of 243 was attained. The limits of detection and quantification achieved under optimal conditions for bromide were found to be 1.4 and 4.4µgL−1, respectively. The intra-day repeatability, expressed as relative standard deviation, was 4.4% (n=6). Besides, the inter-day reproducibility, performed at four different days, was 7.1%. Finally, the developed method was successfully applied to the determination of bromide in different water samples, showing recovery values in the range of 95–110%, and validated against certified reference material BCR-611 (ground water, Br− low level). The proposed method represents a highly convenient approach for monitoring of bromide at very low concentrations.

ce:section.ce:para: The biological essentiality of bromide for basement membranes assembly and tissue development has been recently demonstrated [1]. Moreover, several studies have demonstrated its low degree of toxicity and reduced toxicological concern in nutrition [2]. However, it has also been reported in the literature that bromide, which is ubiquitously found in water, is a key matrix component in oxidative water treatment processes as it can promote the formation of undesired compounds [3]. The formation of brominated disinfection by-products (DBPs) of concern has, in fact, motivated a variety of studies regarding mechanisms of DBPs formation and toxicity [4–7]. Generated DBPs show adverse impacts to human health at very low concentrations, so analytical methodologies capable of monitoring bromide at trace levels are required.

Standard methods for determination of bromide in water samples involve ion chromatography, capillary ion electrophoresis with indirect UV detection and UV–vis spectrophotometry [8]. In addition, a number of alternative methodologies involving UV–vis spectrophotometry [9,10], spectrofluorimetry [11], amperometric detection [12], inductively coupled plasma-mass spectrometry [13,14], dielectric barrier discharge-optical emission [15] and total reflexion X-Ray fluorescence [16] have been reported in the literature for bromide determination. It should be noted that flow methods are amply used to on-line monitoring of bromide [9,10,12]. In addition, a microfluidic paper-based analytical device has been recently proposed for on-site detection of high concentrations of bromide in wastewater samples [17]. Development of simple, rapid, sensitive and portable alternatives for determination of bromide at trace level would be highly desirable for in situ field monitoring of this halide.

Miniaturization of sample preparation has significantly contributed to the development of improved analytical methodologies. Among other advantages, microextraction techniques enable the achievement of high enrichment factors along with an efficient clean-up of the sample and negligible consumption of solvents. Single-drop microextraction (SDME) is nowadays a well-established liquid-phase microextraction (LPME) technique that exploits the use of a very high sample-to- solvent ratio to achieve the preconcentration of target compounds in a drop of extractant phase, which typically ranges from 1 to 5μL [18,19]. The headspace mode (HS-SDME) is an excellent approach for the enrichment of volatile analytes or analyte derivatives that also provides increased selectivity, since non-volatile compounds remain in the sample solution and are therefore not extracted.

Some remarkable developments on the combination of HS-SDME with microvolume detection systems have been recently reported towards the automated determination of a variety of analytes [20–22]. However, in spite of the huge potential of miniaturized sample preparation techniques, their coupling with analytical instrumentation is sometimes troublesome. For instance, the development of analytical methodologies based on the combination of LPME with fluorescence spectrometry has been scarcely exploited. In fact, the lack of compatibility between the volumes typically required to carry our microextraction processes and fluorescence measurements forced researchers to use relatively large extractant phase volumes [23] or dilute the enriched extract prior to perform the analysis [24,25]. The current availability of microvolume fluorospectrometers opens up great possibilities for the development of sensitive methodologies in combination with microextraction approaches since dilution is avoided. This fact enables achieving extremely high enrichment factors, as recently demonstrated [26–31].

In this work, we describe a novel methodology for determination of bromide at trace levels by coupling HS-SDME and microvolume fluorospectrometry. The method involves in situ bromine generation, transfer of the volatile to the headspace and simultaneous trapping/reaction onto a fluorescein-containing aqueous microdrop. A decrease in the fluorescence intensity is thus produced as a result of the in-drop reaction occurring between bromine and fluorescein to yield the less fluorescent eosin. Fluorescence parameters, vapor bromine generation and transfer conditions, as well as extractant phase composition were fully assessed, and the applicability of the method to the analysis of water samples was demonstrated.
All chemicals used were of analytical reagent grade. An Ultra Clear™ TWF EDI water purification system (Siemens, Barsbettel, Germany) was used to obtain ultrapure water.

A stock standard solution of bromide (1000mgL−1) was prepared from potassium bromide (Sigma-Aldrich, St. Louis, MO, USA). Working standards were daily made by dilution of the stock solution with ultrapure water. Potassium bromate (Sigma-Aldrich) and sulfuric acid (Prolabo, Paris, France) were used for bromine generation. Fluorescein (Panreac, Barcelona, Spain) was used for in-drop sensing of vapor bromine, and two water-miscible organic solvents, namely, absolute ethanol (Prolabo) and N,N-dimethylformamide (Merck, Darmstadt, Germany), were evaluated in microextraction experiments.

The following reagents were used to evaluate potential interferences: NaCl, KI, CdO and FeCl3·6H2O from Sigma-Aldrich; KIO3 and CuCl2·2H2O from Merck; NaNO2 and Ni(NO3)2·6H2O from Panreac; NaHCO3 and Na2SO4 from Carlo Erba (Milan, Italy), NaHClO from Prolabo; and KNO3 from Probus (Badalona, Spain).

Fluorescence measurements were carried out with a Thermo Scientific NanoDrop 3300 Fluorospectrometer (Wilmington, DE, USA). Three solid-state light emitting diodes (LEDs) are available as excitation sources, being perpendicularly oriented to the detector. A 2048-element charge-coupled device array detector was connected by an optical fiber to the optical measurement surface. Fluorescence measurements were carried out by a cuvetteless sample retention technology based in surface tension. Fluorescence measurements were carried out at 513nm by using the blue LED as excitation source (470nm). The optical pathlength was set at 1mm.

A 10μL high precision microsyringe (Hamilton model 1701 RN, 10 AL) was used. Microextraction experiments were performed using 40mL amber-vials with screw caps of 24mm (Supelco, Bellefonte, USA) and PTFE-faced septa of 22mm (Supelco).

Blanks, standards or samples (10mL) made in 0.75molL−1 H2SO4 were placed into a 40-mL amber vial together with a stir bar. After closing the sample vial, 1mL of a 5mmolL−1 solution of KBrO3 was externally injected to generate vapor bromine. Then, 2μL of extractant phase (20 μmol L−1 fluorescein aqueous solution containing DMF 2% (v/v) and adjusted to pH 10 with NaOH) was exposed to the headspace above a sample stirred at 700rpm for 10min. Once the microextraction process was finished, the enriched drop was retracted back into the syringe and subsequently deposited between the sample pedestals of the microvolume fluorospectrometer.

The method proposed in this work involves chemical conversion of bromide into vapor bromine, its mass transfer to the headspace and subsequent reaction with a fluorescein-containing aqueous microdrop. The chemical reactions involved are as follows:
(1) 5Br−+BrO3 −+6H+→3Br2+3H2O
(2) 4Br2+fluorescein→eosin+4Br−

Specifically, bromide is converted into vapor bromine by redox reaction with bromate in acidic media (reaction 1), whereas bromination of fluorescein takes place when the formed volatile reacts with a fluorescein-containing extractant phase, thus yielding tetrabromofluorescein (eosin) (reaction 2). As a result of the in-drop reaction, a decrease in the fluorescence intensity is produced. A number of experimental parameters influencing the microextraction approach have been evaluated for optimal performance, the obtained results being discussed in this section.

Fluorescence parameters were initially evaluated to achieve the highest sensitivity. Three solid-state light emitting diodes (LEDs), namely UV, blue and white, can be selected as excitation sources in the microvolume fluorospectrometer used in this work, thus covering the range 365–650nm. The excitation wavelength was evaluated by using the three available LEDs to obtain the fluorescence emission spectra of a 5µmolL−1 fluorescein-containing aqueous drop. As can be observed from Fig. 1, the blue LED (470±10nm) provided the highest sensitivity for the fluorophore, so it was selected as the optimal excitation source for further experiments. The excitation and emission wavelengths were set at 470 and 513nm, respectively.

Selection of the appropriate extractant phase is of utmost importance in HS-SDME. Thus, those experimental parameters influencing the extractability of vapor bromine and its subsequent reaction with fluorescein were evaluated, namely, fluorescein concentration, type and concentration of miscible organic modifiers, and pH of the drop. The concentration of fluorescein in the drop was firstly evaluated in order to ensure the highest linear dynamic range. The fluorescence intensity of fluorescein showed a linear relationship with the fluorescein concentration up to 20µmolL−1 of the fluorophore, so this concentration was set as optimal. Under these conditions, microextraction experiments were carried out to ascertain the effect of the presence of a miscible organic solvent in the drop with the aim of improving the extraction of vapor bromine. Two water-miscible solvents showing different physicochemical properties were evaluated as organic modifiers of the extractant phase. Thus, ethanol and N,N-dimethylformamide (DMF) were set at a fixed concentration of 4% (v/v), and HS-SDME experiments were carried out for comparison purposes. According to the obtained results, DMF yielded a 4-fold enhancement of the analytical signal when compared with ethanol, as well as lower blanks. These results could be possibly ascribed to the higher similarity of the Hansen solubility parameters of DMF to the ones of bromine as compared to ethanol, as well as to the reduction of the resonance of the aromatic cycles by hydrogen bonding interactions in xanthene ring [32]. According to these results, DMF was selected for further experiments. The impact of DMF concentration in the drop was subsequently evaluated, the results of the study being shown in Fig. 2A. As can be observed from the figure, the analytical signal increased with an increase of the DMF content in the range of 0.6–2.0% (v/v), whereas a further increase in DMF concentration in the drop gave rise to a slight signal decrease. High blanks and a bathochromic effect, corresponding to a different stabilization of ground and excitation estates of fluorescein [33], were also observed at higher DMF concentrations, so a 2% (v/v) DMF content in the drop was selected as optimal. Finally, the effect of pH of the extractant phase was assessed. Fluorescein may exist in different prototropic forms (cation, neutral, monoanioninc and dianionic) in solution, the dianionic form showing the highest fluorescence intensity with a quantum yield of 0.93 [34]. Thus, microextraction experiments were carried out to evaluate the impact of pH of the extractant phase over the analytical response. The results obtained in this study (Fig. 2B) show that the analytical signal significantly increased with the pH of the drop in the range of 7–10, while a decrease in the signal was observed upon further increase of pH. A pH value of 10 was selected as it yielded the highest sensitivity for determination of bromide.

An efficient generation of vapor bromine is needed to achieve the highest sensitivity. Bromine is commonly generated by oxidation of bromide in acidic media by means of a weak oxidizing agent [35]. Sulfuric acid and potassium bromate were selected to generate vapor bromine as described in the literature [10]. The effect of the sulfuric acid concentration on the generation of vapor bromine was assessed in the range of 0.1–1.5molL−1. As can be seen in Fig. 3A, the analytical signal drastically increased with increasing the acid concentration in the aqueous phase up to 0.75molL−1. The use of higher concentrations of sulfuric acid resulted in worse blanks and a decrease in the analytical response. Based on these results, a sulfuric acid concentration of 0.75molL−1 was chosen as it provided a good signal-to-blank ratio and a relatively reduced acid consumption per sample. Next, the effect of potassium bromate concentration was evaluated. Its effect on the in situ bromine generation was evaluated in the range of 0.1–0.7mmolL−1, and the obtained results are provided in Fig. 3B. As can be observed in the figure, the analytical response increased in the whole studied range. The use of higher bromate concentrations in microextraction experiments was not attempted as the blank values increased significantly and the precision was worsened. A bromate concentration of 0.5mmolL−1 was finally selected keeping in mind the good sensitivity, precision and signal-to-blank ratio achieved under these conditions.

The microextraction process involves bromine generation, its transfer to the headspace and subsequent trapping of the volatile by an aqueous microdrop, where it reacts with fluorescein. Thus, temperature, sample volume and microextraction time were evaluated in this work in order to obtain the highest sensitivity for bromide determination. The effect of temperature on the microextraction process was studied in the range 2–60 ᵒC. As can be seen in Fig. 4A, the analytical signal sharply increased by increasing the temperature from 2 to 12°C, whereas a less pronounced enhancement of the sensitivity was observed up to 40°C. Above this temperature, a drastic decrease in the analytical response together with higher blanks, was observed. According to the obtained results, experiments were carried out at room temperature (ca. 20°C) for simplicity. Next, the effect of sample volume was studied in the range of 5–20mL (Fig. 4B). An almost linear increase on the analytical signal was observed by increasing the sample volume from 5 to 15mL, whereas the use of higher volumes resulted in a reduced analytical response. A sample volume of 10mL was selected for further experiments as it yielded good sensitivity and precision. Finally, the microextraction time was studied. The results shown in Fig. 4C indicate that the highest analytical response was achieved when a microextraction time of 10min was used. Blanks significantly worsened at extended extraction times, giving rise to a decrease in the analytical signal. Thus, a microextraction time of 10min was selected as it provided the highest sensitivity and an acceptable sample throughput.

The effect of potential interferences on the proposed analytical method was evaluated using a 15μgL−1 solution of bromide. Interference effects were considered to be significant when a signal variation beyond ±10% was produced. The obtained results indicated that the method was not affected in the presence of 500000μgL−1 sulfate, 100000μgL−1 nitrate, 15000μgL−1 carbonate, 10000μgL−1 chloride, 750μgL−1 hypochlorite, 200μgL−1 iodate, 10μgL−1 nitrite and 1μgL−1 iodide. The proposed method as well as alternative methodologies for bromide determination showed a reduced tolerance ratio for iodide and nitrite [10,36]. In spite of that, the iodide/bromide and nitrite/bromide ratios in water samples are typically lower than the corresponding tolerance ratio of the proposed method [10,37]. Metal ions were also evaluated as potential interferences showing that 250μgL−1 Fe (III), 500μgL−1 Cd (II), 500μgL−1 Cu (II) and 1000μgL−1 Ni (II) had no influence on the analytical response.

The analytical characteristics of the proposed method were evaluated under optimal conditions. The equation for the calibration graph was F0−F=6711.7 [Br−]+384.1, where F0−F is the relative change in fluorescence intensity and [Br−] is the concentration of bromide in μg L−1. The calibration graph was linear in the range 5 and 25μgL−1, with a regression coefficient was 0.9992.

The limits of detection (LOD) and quantification (LOQ) were calculated according to IUPAC recommendations as 3.3s/m and 10s/m, respectively (s being the standard deviation of 10 blank measurements and m the slope of calibration curve). The LOD of the proposed method was 1.4μgL−1, whereas the LOQ was 4.4μgL−1. The intra-day repeatability, expressed as relative standard deviation, was calculated by consecutively extracting six aqueous samples spiked at the 15μgL−1 concentration level, and found to be 4.4%. Besides, the inter-day reproducibility, performed at four different days, was 7.1%.

The enrichment factor, defined as the ratio between the analyte concentration in the final extract and its initial concentration in the sample [38], was estimated bearing in mind the stoichiometry of the chemical reactions (1 and 2) involved in the proposed method (bromide oxidation and subsequent bromination of fluorescein). Specifically, the analyte concentration in the drop was calculated taking into account the number of moles of fluorescein that reacted with vapor bromine under optimal HS-SDME conditions, which was accompanied by a decrease of the fluorescence intensity of the fluorescein-containing microdrop. An enrichment factor of 243 was achieved.

The analytical characteristics of the proposed methodology were compared with the ones of alternative methods reported in the literature. As can be observed from Table 1, the proposed method provides an excellent LOD for bromide determination. It is worth noting that the combination of LPME techniques with GC-MS [39] and the coupling PVG-ICP-MS [14] enable bromide detection well below the ppb level. Nevertheless, the proposed method involves portable analytical systems and can thus be employed for rapid on-site analysis of water samples with an acceptable repeatability and reproducibility.

Finally, the proposed method was applied to the analysis of different aqueous samples. Bromide was detected, yet below the LOQ of the method, in one of the analyzed samples (bottled mineral water), whereas bromide concentrations of 59±3μgL−1 and 62±5μgL−1 were obtained in drinking fountain water and tap water samples respectively, after 10-fold dilution. Recovery studies were also carried out at two bromide concentration levels to check for potential matrix effects ( Table 2). Recoveries were found in the range 95–110%, thus demonstrating the applicability of the method for bromide determination in water samples. Also, the analytical method was validated against certified reference material BCR-611 (ground water, certified concentration of Br-: 93±4μgkg−1). No significant differences were observed between the found concentration (96±8μgkg−1, n=5) and the certified concentration value (texp<tcrit, p 0.05, 2-tailed test, 4f.d).

In situ bromine generation followed by the enrichment of the volatile by a fluorescein-containing aqueous microdrop has shown excellent performance for bromide enrichment prior to fluorimetric analysis. Remarkably, the volumes needed to perform both the enrichment and subsequent fluorescence measurements are fully compatible so dilution is avoided, and hence, the combination of HS-SDME and microvolume fluorospectrometry enables a highly sensitive analysis. The herein reported coupling provides a convenient alternative to previously reported methods for a sensitive bromide determination in water samples, being also amenable for field analysis.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Financial support from the Spanish Ministry of Economy and Competitiveness (Project CTQ2015-68146-P) (MINECO/FEDER) is gratefully acknowledged.
F. Pena-Pereira thanks Xunta de Galicia for financial support as a post-doctoral researcher of the I2C program.
